terms.name: UNITID
terms.definition: A unique 6-digit identifier assigned to every postsecondary institution by the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System (IPEDS). Stable across reporting years and authoritative for institution identity.

terms.name: Institution Name
terms.definition: The official name of a postsecondary institution as reported to IPEDS. Multi-campus systems may share the same name across distinct UNITIDs.

terms.name: CIP Code
terms.definition: A Classification of Instructional Programs code that categorizes academic programs at postsecondary institutions. Standard format is XX.XXXX (2-digit family code followed by a dot and 4-digit detail code). Defined by the NCES CIP 2020 taxonomy.

terms.name: Program Name
terms.definition: The human-readable description of an academic program corresponding to its CIP code, as defined in the NCES CIP taxonomy. Each CIP code maps to exactly one program description.

terms.name: CIP Family
terms.definition: The 2-digit prefix of a CIP code representing the broad discipline area (e.g., 52 = Business, Management, Marketing). Derived by extracting the first two characters of the normalized XX.XXXX CIP code.

terms.name: CIP Family Name
terms.definition: The human-readable label for a 2-digit CIP family code, describing the broad discipline area (e.g., 'Business, Management, Marketing, and Related Support Services' for CIP family 52).

terms.name: Credential Level
terms.definition: An integer classification code indicating the type of credential awarded by a program. Values: 1=Undergraduate Certificate, 2=Associate's, 3=Bachelor's, 4=Post-baccalaureate Certificate, 5=Master's, 6=Doctoral, 7=First Professional, 8=Graduate Certificate. Defined by the College Scorecard data dictionary.

terms.name: Credential Description
terms.definition: The human-readable label for a credential level code (e.g., 'Bachelor's Degree' for CREDLEV=3). Defined in the College Scorecard data dictionary.

terms.name: Median Earnings 1-Year Post-Completion
terms.definition: The median earnings (high estimate) of graduates who are working and not enrolled in further education, measured 1 year after program completion. This is a cohort-level aggregate, not an individual measure. The 1-year and 2-year figures come from different cohort measurement windows and do NOT represent the same individuals tracked over time.

terms.name: Median Earnings 2-Year Post-Completion
terms.definition: The median earnings (high estimate) of graduates who are working and not enrolled in further education, measured 2 years after program completion. This is a cohort-level aggregate from a different measurement window than the 1-year figure. The 2-year value may be lower than the 1-year value because they represent different graduating cohorts, not longitudinal tracking.

terms.name: Median Debt at Completion
terms.definition: The median cumulative federal loan debt of students who completed a program, including all student groups evaluated. Represents the typical total borrowing for graduates of a specific program at a specific institution.

terms.name: IPEDS Completions Count
terms.definition: The number of students completing a program as reported to the Integrated Postsecondary Education Data System. IPEDSCOUNT1 and IPEDSCOUNT2 represent two different measurement windows (first major and second major, respectively). The counts are highly correlated (r=0.984) but not identical.

terms.name: Privacy Suppression
terms.definition: The U.S. Department of Education's practice of replacing outcome data (earnings, debt) with null values when program cohort sizes are too small to protect student privacy under FERPA. The effective threshold is approximately 30 completers: programs with 30+ completers have 88.7% earnings data availability, while programs below 10 completers have under 11%. Suppressed values appear as null in the dataset.
terms.source_reference: governance/domain-context.md (Edge Cases: Privacy suppression); FERPA disclosure avoidance rules

terms.name: Small Cohort Flag
terms.definition: A derived boolean flag indicating that a program has fewer than 30 completers (completions_count_1 < 30), meaning its outcome data is likely subject to privacy suppression and should be interpreted with caution. Programs are flagged but not excluded from the dataset.

terms.name: Record ID
terms.definition: A deterministic hash-based identifier computed from the grain fields (unitid, cipcode, credlev) using the Brightsmith compute_grain_id() function. Provides a stable, unique surrogate key for each row in the base table. Prefixed with 'cs' for the College Scorecard domain.

terms.name: Source Load Date
terms.definition: The date on which the source data was loaded into the pipeline's raw zone. Represents when the data was fetched from the U.S. Department of Education, not when the underlying outcomes were measured.

terms.name: Ingestion Timestamp
terms.definition: The timestamp recording when a row was written to the Silver zone base table. Used for pipeline auditing and data freshness tracking. Generated at Silver zone transformation time.

terms.name: Cross-Institution Earnings Percentile Band
terms.definition: A distributional summary (25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles) of program-level median earnings across all institutions within the same CIP family. Computed via window functions (PERCENTILE_CONT) over the set of non-null program medians. These bands answer the question 'what range of earnings outcomes can I expect for a given field of study across different schools?' They are NOT within-cohort percentiles of individual earners — they are percentiles of institutional program medians. CIP families with fewer than 3 non-null values have their bands set to null (insufficient sample).
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-career-outcomes-college-scorecard.md (Derived: Percentile Bands section; Source Data Constraint section)

terms.name: Debt-to-Earnings Ratio (Annual)
terms.definition: The ratio of median cumulative student debt to median first-year post-completion earnings for a given program at a given institution (debt_median / earnings_1yr_median). A key affordability metric: values below 1.0 indicate debt is less than one year of earnings (manageable), while values above 2.0 indicate debt significantly exceeds first-year earnings (concerning). Null when either debt or earnings data is missing. Conceptually related to the Department of Education's Gainful Employment debt-to-earnings test, though GE uses annual loan payments rather than total debt.
terms.source_reference: https://www.federalregister.gov/documents/2023/10/10/2023-21534/financial-value-transparency-and-gainful-employment (Gainful Employment Final Rule, 34 CFR 668); docs/specs/gold-career-outcomes-college-scorecard.md (Derived: Financial Ratios)

terms.name: Debt-to-Earnings Tier
terms.definition: A categorical classification of the debt-to-earnings ratio into four buckets: 'Low' (ratio < 0.75, debt very manageable), 'Moderate' (0.75 to 1.5, typical range), 'High' (1.5 to 2.5, may require extended repayment), 'Very High' (ratio >= 2.5, debt significantly exceeds first-year earnings). Null when the underlying ratio is null. Threshold values are informed by Department of Education Gainful Employment guidance and industry convention.

terms.name: Cross-Cohort Earnings Differential
terms.definition: The relative difference in median earnings between the 1-year and 2-year post-completion measurement windows, computed as (earnings_2yr_median - earnings_1yr_median) / earnings_1yr_median. This is a cross-cohort comparison, NOT longitudinal tracking: the 1-year and 2-year figures come from different graduating cohorts measured at different times, so this metric reflects differences between cohort snapshots rather than earnings growth of the same individuals. Negative values are common and expected — approximately 44% of programs show 2-year cohort earnings below 1-year cohort earnings due to cohort composition differences. Null when either earnings value is missing.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-career-outcomes-college-scorecard.md (Derived: Financial Ratios); governance/domain-context.md (Temporal Patterns: Measurement windows)

terms.name: CIP Family Earnings Rank
terms.definition: A program's relative position within its CIP family based on 1-year median earnings, computed via PERCENT_RANK() window function partitioned by CIP family and ordered by earnings_1yr_median. Values range from 0.0 (lowest earner in the family) to 1.0 (highest earner). Null when earnings data is missing. Enables comparison of a specific school's program outcomes against the full distribution of programs in the same discipline.

terms.name: Program Value Index
terms.definition: A return-on-investment proxy computed as earnings_1yr_median / debt_median. Higher values indicate better value — more earnings relative to debt incurred. For example, a program with $50,000 median earnings and $25,000 median debt has a program value index of 2.0. This is the mathematical inverse of the Debt-to-Earnings Ratio (BT-019); higher PVI = lower DTE. Null when either earnings or debt data is missing. This is a simplified ROI measure that does not account for time value of money, opportunity cost, or non-financial benefits of education.

terms.name: Confidence Tier
terms.definition: A four-level data quality classification assigned to every row in the career outcomes table, based on cohort size and data availability. Tiers: 'high' (large cohort with both earnings and debt data), 'medium' (large cohort with either earnings or debt), 'low' (small cohort with some outcome data), 'insufficient' (no earnings or debt data available). Determines how much trust downstream consumers should place in a program's outcome metrics. Every row receives a tier — there are no nulls.

terms.name: Outcome Completeness
terms.definition: A numeric score representing the proportion of core outcome fields (earnings_1yr_median, earnings_2yr_median, debt_median) that are non-null for a given program row. Computed as count of non-null fields divided by 3. Possible values are exactly 0.0 (no outcome data), 0.33 (one field present), 0.67 (two fields present), or 1.0 (all three fields present). Provides a quick measure of how complete a program's outcome profile is.

terms.name: Promotion Timestamp
terms.definition: The timestamp recording when a row was promoted from the Silver zone to the Gold zone consumable table. Used for pipeline auditing, data freshness tracking, and debugging. Generated at Gold zone promotion time. Analogous to the Silver zone's ingested_at field but marks the transition to the consumable layer.

terms.name: SOC Code
terms.definition: A Standard Occupational Classification code in XX-XXXX format that uniquely identifies an occupation within the SOC 2018 taxonomy. The first two digits denote the major group (e.g., 15 = Computer and Mathematical). Used by all U.S. federal statistical agencies for occupation classification. In the FutureProof pipeline, SOC code is the primary join key linking BLS employment projections to O*NET task data (direct join) and to College Scorecard programs (via the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk).

terms.name: Occupation Title
terms.definition: The official BLS name for an occupation as published in the Employment Projections and Occupational Outlook Handbook. Each SOC code maps to exactly one occupation title. Examples: 'Software Developers' (15-1252), 'Registered Nurses' (29-1141).
terms.source_reference: https://www.bls.gov/ooh/ (Bureau of Labor Statistics, Occupational Outlook Handbook)

terms.name: SOC Major Group
terms.definition: The 2-digit prefix of a SOC code identifying one of 22 major occupation groups in the SOC 2018 taxonomy (e.g., 11 = Management, 15 = Computer and Mathematical, 29 = Healthcare Practitioners and Technical). Derived by extracting the first two characters of the XX-XXXX SOC code. Used for aggregation and fallback grouping when detailed crosswalk matches fail.

terms.name: SOC Major Group Name
terms.definition: The human-readable label for a 2-digit SOC major group code, describing the broad occupation family (e.g., 'Management' for code 11, 'Computer and Mathematical' for code 15). There are 22 major group names in the SOC 2018 taxonomy.

terms.name: Employment Current
terms.definition: The total number of workers employed in an occupation during the base year of the BLS projection cycle (2023 in the current 2023-2033 cycle). Reported by BLS in thousands in the source data; converted to actual counts by the Bronze zone ingestor (multiplied by 1,000 and rounded to long integer). Represents a point-in-time employment estimate, not seasonally adjusted.

terms.name: Employment Projected
terms.definition: The projected total number of workers employed in an occupation at the end of the BLS 10-year projection horizon (2033 in the current 2023-2033 cycle). Converted from thousands to actual counts in the Bronze zone. Used together with Employment Current to compute employment change and growth rate.

terms.name: Employment Change
terms.definition: The absolute change in employment for an occupation over the BLS 10-year projection cycle, computed as employment_projected minus employment_current. Can be negative for declining occupations (232 of 832 occupations, or 27.9%, are projected to decline in the current cycle). Converted from thousands to actual counts in the Bronze zone.

terms.name: Employment Change Percent
terms.definition: The percentage change in employment for an occupation over the BLS 10-year projection cycle. Can be negative for declining occupations. The average across all occupations is typically 3-5%. This field backs the GRW stat in the Gold zone and is the input for the derived Growth Category classification. Range in current data: -36.1% to +49.9%.

terms.name: Annual Average Openings
terms.definition: The projected annual average number of job openings for an occupation, combining growth openings (new positions from expanding employment) and replacement openings (filling positions vacated by workers leaving the occupation). Always non-negative even for declining occupations, because replacement needs persist regardless of growth trend. Four very small occupations show zero due to BLS rounding from thousands. Converted from thousands to actual counts in the Bronze zone.

terms.name: Median Annual Wage (Occupation)
terms.definition: The BLS-reported median annual wage for an occupation. Half of workers in the occupation earn more and half earn less. Subject to top-coding at $239,200 for occupations where the true median exceeds the BLS confidentiality threshold (though the current interactive export uses actual values with no capped wages observed). Null for 23 occupations where wage data is not available (elected officials, self-employed-dominated occupations). This field backs the ERN stat in the Gold zone.
terms.source_reference: https://www.bls.gov/oes/ (BLS Occupational Employment and Wage Statistics)

terms.name: Median Wage Capped
terms.definition: A boolean flag indicating whether the BLS top-coded the median annual wage at $239,200 because the true median exceeds the BLS confidentiality threshold. True means the original BLS value was '>=239,200' and the actual median is higher than reported. In the current interactive export, all values are False (0 capped wages) because the interactive format uses actual numeric values. Preserved in Silver to protect against future data source format changes.

terms.name: Education Code (BLS)
terms.definition: A BLS integer classification code (1-8) indicating the typical entry-level education requirement for an occupation. Values: 1=Doctoral or professional degree, 2=Master's degree, 3=Bachelor's degree, 4=Associate's degree, 5=Postsecondary nondegree award, 6=Some college no degree, 7=High school diploma or equivalent, 8=No formal educational credential. Derived from BLS text labels in the Bronze zone ingestor.
terms.source_reference: https://www.bls.gov/emp/tables/education-and-training.htm (BLS Employment Projections, Education and Training Classification)

terms.name: Education Level Name
terms.definition: A normalized human-readable label for the BLS education code, derived by lookup from the education code integer. Ensures consistent labeling across the pipeline. Values: 'Doctoral or professional degree' (code 1), 'Master's degree' (2), 'Bachelor's degree' (3), 'Associate's degree' (4), 'Postsecondary nondegree award' (5), 'Some college, no degree' (6), 'High school diploma or equivalent' (7), 'No formal educational credential' (8).
terms.source_reference: https://www.bls.gov/emp/tables/education-and-training.htm (BLS Employment Projections, Education and Training Classification)

terms.name: Broad Occupation Flag
terms.definition: A derived boolean flag identifying 7 SOC codes in the BLS Employment Projections that represent rolled-up/broad occupation categories rather than individual detailed occupations. These codes aggregate multiple detailed O*NET occupations under a single BLS entry (e.g., 13-1020 combines 13-1021 and 13-1022). The 7 known broad codes are: 13-1020, 13-2020, 29-2010, 31-1120, 39-7010, 47-4090, 51-2090. Flagged because they require special handling in the CIP-to-SOC crosswalk (may map to multiple O*NET children) and should carry lower confidence in downstream career guidance.

terms.name: Growth Category
terms.definition: A derived categorical classification that buckets an occupation's employment_change_pct into one of six human-readable tiers: 'declining_fast' (< -10%), 'declining' (-10% to -1%), 'stable' (-1% to +1%), 'growing' (+1% to +10%), 'growing_fast' (+10% to +20%), 'booming' (>= +20%). Null when employment_change_pct is null. Thresholds are based on BLS convention where average 10-year growth across all occupations is typically 3-5%. This field enables intuitive career guidance about occupation growth prospects.

terms.name: Wage Available
terms.definition: A derived boolean convenience flag indicating whether median_annual_wage is not null for an occupation. True for 809 of 832 occupations; False for 23 occupations where BLS does not report wage data (elected officials, self-employed-dominated occupations). Provides a simple filter for downstream queries that require wage data without needing to check for nulls directly.

terms.name: Catchall Flag
terms.definition: A derived boolean flag identifying occupations whose title contains the substring 'all other' (case-insensitive), indicating a BLS residual category that groups occupations not individually classified within a SOC minor group. Approximately 46 occupations are flagged. These are legitimate BLS categories with real employment and wage data, but they are inherently heterogeneous (e.g., 'Managers, all other' could represent any unlisted management specialty). Career guidance generated from catchall categories should carry lower confidence because the occupation mapping is less specific.

terms.name: Work Experience Code (BLS)
terms.definition: A BLS integer classification code (1-3) indicating the typical related work experience requirement for entry into an occupation. Values: 1=5 years or more, 2=Less than 5 years, 3=None. Derived from BLS text labels in the Bronze zone ingestor. 86.1% of occupations require no prior related work experience (code 3).
terms.source_reference: https://www.bls.gov/emp/tables/education-and-training.htm (BLS Employment Projections, Education and Training Classification)

terms.name: Training Code (BLS)
terms.definition: A BLS integer classification code (1-6) indicating the typical on-the-job training requirement for an occupation. Values: 1=Internship/residency, 2=Apprenticeship, 3=Long-term on-the-job training, 4=Moderate-term on-the-job training, 5=Short-term on-the-job training, 6=None. Derived from BLS text labels in the Bronze zone ingestor. 38.7% of occupations require no on-the-job training (code 6).
terms.source_reference: https://www.bls.gov/emp/tables/education-and-training.htm (BLS Employment Projections, Education and Training Classification)

terms.name: Projection Cycle
terms.definition: The 10-year time span covered by a BLS Employment Projections release (e.g., '2023-2033'). BLS releases new projections biennially, and each release is a complete replacement of the prior cycle's data. The base year is the starting year and the target year is the ending year. Employment Current corresponds to the base year and Employment Projected to the target year.

terms.name: GRW Score
terms.definition: A derived growth stat on a 1-10 scale computed from employment_change_pct using a piecewise linear function anchored to BLS growth benchmarks. Maps the national average growth (~4%) to approximately 6.0 on the scale, placing 'average' slightly above the midpoint since growth is a positive outcome. Severe decline (<=20%) maps to 1.0; exceptional growth (>=20%) maps to 9.0-10.0 (capped at 10.0). This score directly backs the GRW pentagon stat in the FutureProof five-stat system. Null only if employment_change_pct is null (0 rows in current data). Also available as grw_score_rounded (integer 1-10 for display).

terms.name: Wage Percentile Overall
terms.definition: An occupation's relative wage position among all occupations with wage data, computed via PERCENT_RANK() ordered by median_annual_wage. Values range from 0.0 (lowest-paying occupation) to 1.0 (highest-paying occupation). Null for 23 occupations where wage data is unavailable. Used as input for wage_tier classification and for relative positioning in the ERN stat and Ceiling boss fight.

terms.name: Wage Percentile Education Tier
terms.definition: An occupation's relative wage position within its education requirement tier, computed via PERCENT_RANK() partitioned by education_code and ordered by median_annual_wage. Shows how well a career pays compared to peers requiring the same level of education. Values range from 0.0 to 1.0. Null for 23 occupations where wage data is unavailable. Backs the Ceiling boss fight by revealing whether an occupation pays well relative to careers with similar education requirements.

terms.name: Wage Tier
terms.definition: A categorical classification of an occupation's wage position based on wage_percentile_overall, bucketed into five tiers: 'low' (percentile < 0.25, bottom quartile earners), 'below_average' (0.25-0.50), 'above_average' (0.50-0.75), 'high' (0.75-0.90, strong earners), 'very_high' (>= 0.90, top 10% of occupations). Null when wage data is unavailable (23 occupations). Provides an intuitive label for career earnings potential.

terms.name: Market Score
terms.definition: A derived composite score on a 1-10 scale combining employment growth direction (60% weight via grw_score) and opportunity volume (40% weight via openings_score, which is PERCENT_RANK of openings_annual_avg mapped to 1-10). Backs the Market boss fight in the FutureProof game engine. The weighting reflects that growth direction matters more than volume, but corrects for occupations growing fast with very few total jobs. Null if grw_score or openings_annual_avg is null. Also available as market_score_rounded (integer 1-10 for display).

terms.name: Occupation Confidence Tier
terms.definition: A three-level data quality classification assigned to every row in the occupation profiles table. 'high': specific occupation with wage data (not broad, not catchall, wage available). 'medium': broad or catchall occupation with wage data (real data but represents heterogeneous group, guidance less specific). 'low': wage data unavailable (regardless of other flags; weakest data for FutureProof since ERN stat cannot be computed). Every row receives a tier. Distinct from the career outcomes confidence tier (BT-024) which uses cohort size and different criteria.

terms.name: Data Completeness (Occupation)
terms.definition: A numeric score representing the proportion of four core occupation fields (median_annual_wage, employment_current, employment_change_pct, openings_annual_avg) that are non-null for a given occupation row. Computed as count of non-null fields divided by 4. Possible values: 0.0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0. In practice, 0.0 is not expected (all occupations have employment data). Provides a quick measure of how complete an occupation's data profile is.

terms.name: FutureProof Stat Mapping
terms.definition: Documentation fields on each Gold row that record which FutureProof pentagon stats and boss fights the row's data feeds. For the occupation profiles table: backs_stats is always 'ERN,GRW' and backs_bosses is always 'Market,Ceiling'. These are static metadata fields that enable downstream consumers (Gemma agent, frontend) to know which game-system elements are backed by this data product without needing to consult external documentation. The full pentagon (ERN, GRW, RES, HMN, plus all 5 bosses) requires additional Gold products from O*NET and the CIP-SOC crosswalk.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-occupation-profiles-bls-ooh.md (FutureProof Stat Mapping documentation fields; FutureProof Integration Notes)

terms.name: O*NET-SOC Code
terms.definition: An extended Standard Occupational Classification code in XX-XXXX.XX format used by O*NET to identify occupations at a finer grain than the BLS SOC taxonomy. The first 6 characters (XX-XXXX) match the BLS SOC code. The 2-digit suffix (.XX) distinguishes O*NET-specific detailed occupations within a single BLS occupation. A .00 suffix indicates the base/only occupation for that BLS SOC; suffixes .01, .02, etc. indicate detailed splits. O*NET 30.2 contains 1,016 codes: 867 base .00 codes and 149 detailed codes across 76 BLS SOCs. Must always be treated as a string, never numeric.

terms.name: Content Model Element ID
terms.definition: A hierarchical identifier for O*NET's Content Model taxonomy that uniquely identifies a measured dimension of work. Format is dotted-hierarchical (e.g., '4.A.1.a.1' for the Work Activity 'Getting Information', '4.C.3.d.1' for the Work Context 'Time Pressure'). Section 4.A contains 41 Generalized Work Activities and section 4.C contains 57 Work Context elements. Each element has a stable ID across O*NET releases.

terms.name: Work Activity Importance
terms.definition: A rating on the O*NET Importance (IM) scale (1.0-5.0) measuring how important a Generalized Work Activity is to a given occupation. Higher values indicate greater importance. All 41 work activities are rated on the IM scale for every occupation. In the Silver zone, when multiple O*NET detailed codes map to one BLS SOC, the importance value is the unweighted average across those details. This metric backs the HMN (Human Edge) stat in the FutureProof system.
terms.source_reference: https://www.onetcenter.org/content.html (O*NET Content Model, Section 4.A — Generalized Work Activities); O*NET Scales Reference (IM scale)

terms.name: Work Context Value
terms.definition: A point-estimate rating from O*NET's Work Context scales measuring the physical, social, or structural characteristics of a work environment for a given occupation. CX scale elements range 1.0-5.0 (55 elements); CT scale elements range 1.0-3.0 (2 elements: Work Schedules and Duration of Typical Work Week). In the Silver zone, only CX and CT point-estimate scales are retained; the CXP/CTP category-percentage rows (~82% of Bronze Work Context data) are excluded. When multiple O*NET detailed codes map to one BLS SOC, the value is the unweighted average across details.
terms.source_reference: https://www.onetcenter.org/content.html (O*NET Content Model, Section 4.C — Work Context); O*NET Scales Reference (CX/CT scales)

terms.name: Burnout Element
terms.definition: A project-specific classification flag identifying approximately 9 O*NET Work Context elements whose values directly signal burnout risk for an occupation. The proposed burnout-relevant elements include Time Pressure, Duration of Typical Work Week, Consequence of Error, Pace Determined by Speed of Equipment, Frequency of Decision Making, Importance of Being Exact or Accurate, Importance of Repeating Same Tasks, Responsibility for Outcomes and Results, and Work Schedules. Higher context values on these elements indicate greater burnout risk. This flag enables the Burnout boss fight formula in the FutureProof game engine to weight these elements most heavily. The exact element list is subject to human approval.

terms.name: Career Transition (Similarity)
terms.definition: A directional relationship between two occupations indicating occupational similarity, derived from O*NET's Related Occupations data. This represents shared skills/knowledge profiles, NOT observed career transitions. 'Software Developer relates to Database Architect' means the jobs share similar skill requirements, not that software developers commonly become database architects. In the Silver zone, relationships are aggregated to BLS SOC level (XX-XXXX) from O*NET detail level (XX-XXXX.XX). Self-references are removed and duplicates are deduplicated keeping the best (lowest) relatedness index. Used for Stage 3 career branching in FutureProof with appropriate caveats about the similarity-vs-transition distinction.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/silver-base-onet.md (Table 4: base.onet_career_transitions); O*NET 30.2 Related Occupations file

terms.name: Relatedness Tier
terms.definition: A three-level classification of inter-occupation relationships from the O*NET Related Occupations file, based on the relatedness index (1-20). 'Primary-Short' (index 1-5) indicates the most closely related occupations. 'Primary-Long' (index 6-10) indicates the next-closest relationships. 'Supplemental' (index 11-20) indicates broader, less specific relationships. This column is part of the O*NET 30.2 data schema and replaces the simpler binary primary/supplemental distinction from earlier O*NET versions.
terms.source_reference: https://www.onetcenter.org/dictionary/30.2/excel/related_occupations.html (O*NET 30.2 Related Occupations file, Relatedness Tier column)

terms.name: Suppress Flag
terms.definition: A derived boolean flag in the Silver zone indicating that one or more contributing Bronze-level data points had O*NET's recommend_suppress = 'Y', signaling the data was collected from a small sample or has unreliable estimates. When multiple O*NET detailed codes are aggregated to a single BLS SOC, the flag is True if ANY contributing row was flagged for suppression. Affects approximately 1.5% of Work Activity rows and 2.5% of Work Context rows. Data is preserved (not dropped) but flagged for downstream consumers to handle appropriately.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/silver-base-onet.md (Tables 2-3: suppress_flag field); O*NET data quality methodology (recommend_suppress field)

terms.name: Multi-Detail Aggregation
terms.definition: The Silver zone process of combining data from multiple O*NET detailed occupation codes (XX-XXXX.XX) into a single BLS SOC occupation (XX-XXXX). Affects 76 BLS SOC codes that have 2 or more O*NET detailed codes (149 detailed codes total). For numeric ratings (importance, context value), the aggregation uses an unweighted arithmetic mean across details. For boolean flags (suppress_flag), aggregation uses logical OR (True if ANY detail is flagged). For titles/descriptions, the .00 base code's values are used as primary, with detail codes' titles concatenated. The multi_detail_flag on base.onet_occupations indicates which BLS SOCs underwent this aggregation.

terms.name: Data Completeness Tier (O*NET)
terms.definition: A three-level classification assigned to each BLS SOC occupation in the O*NET Silver base table, indicating how much O*NET child data (Work Activities, Work Context, Tasks, Related Occupations) is available. 'full' means all 4 data types are present. 'partial' means some but not all are present (approximately 29 occupations — typically newer occupations awaiting full survey coverage). 'none' means no child data exists (the 93 'All Other'/Military residual occupations, which are excluded from Silver). Distinct from BT-053 (Data Completeness for BLS OOH occupation profiles) which uses a numeric score based on different fields.

terms.name: Importance Rank
terms.definition: A derived integer rank (1 to 41) indicating the relative importance of a Work Activity within a specific occupation, where 1 is the most important activity and 41 is the least important. Computed by ranking all 41 Generalized Work Activities for each occupation by their importance (IM scale) value in descending order. Enables quick identification of an occupation's most and least critical human activities without comparing raw importance scores. Used for the HMN stat profiling in the FutureProof system.

terms.name: HMN Score (Human Edge)
terms.definition: A derived occupation-level stat on a 1-10 scale measuring how much an occupation depends on distinctly human skills that AI struggles to replicate — interpersonal judgment, creative problem-solving, physical presence, emotional intelligence, leadership. Computed as 1.0 + 9.0 * (sum of importance values for human-intensive activities / sum of importance values for all 41 activities). HMN 10.0 means all important work is human-intensive; HMN 1.0 means all important work is automatable. Null for occupations without activity profile data (approximately 24 partial-data occupations). Also available as hmn_score_rounded (integer 1-10 for pentagon display). This score directly backs the HMN pentagon stat in the FutureProof five-stat system.

terms.name: Human-Intensive Activity
terms.definition: A project-specific static classification applied to approximately 14 of the 41 O*NET Generalized Work Activities, identifying activities that require judgment, creativity, interpersonal skill, or physical presence — capabilities that are hard for AI to replicate. The classification is the same for all occupations (it classifies the activity itself, not per-occupation). Examples include 'Guiding, Directing, and Motivating Subordinates' (leadership), 'Thinking Creatively' (creativity), 'Assisting and Caring for Others' (empathy). Activities not classified as human-intensive are considered 'automatable' for HMN score computation. This classification is the most subjective decision in the pipeline and is flagged for adversarial auditor review and human approval.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-onet-profiles.md (Step 1: Classify the 41 work activities; Open Decisions #1)

terms.name: Burnout Score
terms.definition: A derived occupation-level stat on a 1-10 scale measuring burnout risk based on 9 O*NET Work Context elements (Time Pressure, Duration of Typical Work Week, Consequence of Error, Pace Determined by Speed of Equipment, Frequency of Decision Making, Importance of Being Exact or Accurate, Responsibility for Outcomes and Results, Importance of Repeating Same Tasks, Responsibility for Others' Health and Safety). Computed by normalizing each element to 0-1 (CX: (value-1)/4, CT: (value-1)/2), averaging all 9, then mapping to 1-10 scale: 1.0 + 9.0 * average. Burnout 10.0 means maximum on all indicators; Burnout 1.0 means minimum risk. Null for occupations without context profile data (approximately 24 partial-data occupations). Also available as burnout_score_rounded (integer 1-10 for boss fight display). Backs the Burnout boss fight in FutureProof.

terms.name: Burnout Driver
terms.definition: The top 3 contributing Work Context elements to an occupation's burnout score, stored as a JSON array with element names and their normalized values. Powers Gemma's burnout narrative by explaining WHY an occupation has its burnout score (e.g., 'High time pressure, long work hours, and high consequence of error'). Derived by ranking the 9 burnout-relevant context elements by their normalized contribution and selecting the top 3. Null when burnout_score is null.

terms.name: Work Profile
terms.definition: An occupation-level Gold data product that consolidates all O*NET activity and context data for a single occupation into one row. Includes the HMN score, Burnout score, top activity summaries, individual burnout element values, and data quality indicators. One work profile exists per BLS SOC code (798 total). The work profile pivots Silver-zone data (41 activity rows + 57 context rows per occupation) into a single consumable record with pre-computed scores and JSON summaries for downstream consumers (Gemma agent, frontend, stat engine).

terms.name: Work Profile Confidence Tier
terms.definition: A three-level data quality classification assigned to every row in the consumable.onet_work_profiles table. 'high': data_completeness_tier is 'full' AND both activity and context suppression rates are below 5%. 'medium': data_completeness_tier is 'full' but one or both suppression rates are 5% or above. 'low': data_completeness_tier is 'partial' (occupation lacks full O*NET survey coverage). Distinct from BT-024 (career outcomes confidence, 4 levels based on cohort size) and BT-052 (occupation confidence, 3 levels based on wage availability and broad/catchall flags).

terms.name: Activity Importance Mean
terms.definition: The arithmetic mean of all 41 O*NET Work Activity importance (IM scale, 1-5) values for a given occupation. Measures the overall 'activity intensity' of an occupation — how many activities are rated as highly important. Higher values indicate occupations involving many different important activities; lower values indicate more focused occupations. Null when the occupation has no activity profile data. Used as a summary statistic in the work profile alongside the more specific HMN score.

terms.name: CIP-SOC Crosswalk
terms.definition: An official many-to-many mapping table published jointly by NCES and BLS that links Classification of Instructional Programs (CIP) codes to Standard Occupational Classification (SOC) codes. Each row represents a single CIP-SOC pairing, meaning 'this academic program typically prepares graduates for this occupation.' The mapping is based on expert judgment by NCES and BLS statisticians, NOT empirical data on actual graduate employment. One CIP code can map to multiple SOC codes (a program prepares for several careers) and one SOC code can map to multiple CIP codes (an occupation can be reached from several programs). The current version maps CIP 2020 codes to SOC 2018 codes. In the FutureProof pipeline, this crosswalk is the bridge connecting College Scorecard program data to BLS and O*NET occupation data.

terms.name: No Match Sentinel
terms.definition: A special SOC code value of 99-9999 used in the NCES CIP-SOC crosswalk to indicate that a CIP program has no corresponding occupation classification. This signals programs that are not career-oriented or do not map cleanly to specific occupations (e.g., Liberal Arts, General Studies). Rows with soc_code = 99-9999 are excluded from the Silver zone crosswalk table because they carry no join value — they cannot link to BLS or O*NET occupation data. The sentinel is defined by the NCES crosswalk file format, not invented by this project.

terms.name: Join-Readiness Flag
terms.definition: A set of three derived boolean flags on each row of the Silver crosswalk table indicating whether the CIP-SOC pairing can successfully join to each of the three downstream Silver base tables. has_scorecard_match is True if the crosswalk's cipcode exists in base.college_scorecard (meaning actual program-level outcome data is available for this CIP). has_bls_match is True if the crosswalk's soc_code exists in base.bls_ooh (meaning BLS employment projections and wage data are available for this SOC). has_onet_match is True if the crosswalk's soc_code exists in base.onet_occupations (meaning O*NET task/activity/context profile data is available for this SOC). These flags enable downstream consumers to assess data availability before attempting joins, and feed directly into the match_quality classification.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/crosswalk-cip-soc.md (Silver Schema: has_scorecard_match, has_bls_match, has_onet_match fields)

terms.name: Match Quality
terms.definition: A derived categorical classification assigned to every row in the Silver crosswalk table, computed from the three join-readiness flags (has_scorecard_match, has_bls_match, has_onet_match). Five possible values: 'full' (all three flags True — best quality, this CIP-SOC pair connects program data to full occupation data from both BLS and O*NET), 'partial_no_onet' (scorecard and BLS match but no O*NET — missing HMN and Burnout scores), 'partial_no_bls' (scorecard and O*NET match but no BLS — missing growth and market projections), 'scorecard_only' (only scorecard matches — program earnings data available but no occupation-level data), 'no_scorecard' (scorecard does not match — valid crosswalk pair but unreachable by student queries since no school+major data exists). The 'full' quality should be the plurality of rows. This classification drives downstream confidence and determines which FutureProof stats can be computed for a given program-occupation pair.

terms.name: Pentagon Stats
terms.definition: The FutureProof five-stat scoring system that profiles every school+major+career combination across five dimensions on a 1-10 integer scale: Earning Power (ERN), Return on Investment (ROI), AI Resilience (RES), Growth (GRW), and Human Edge (HMN). The five stats are rendered as a pentagon (radar chart) in the frontend. Each stat is computed from different data sources: ERN blends College Scorecard earnings with BLS wages, ROI derives from Scorecard debt-to-earnings, RES is a placeholder pending Karpathy AI exposure scores, GRW comes from BLS employment projections, and HMN comes from O*NET work activity analysis. A value of null means the stat could not be computed due to missing upstream data.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-futureproof-engine.md (The Five Stats section; pentagon-ready schema)

terms.name: Earning Power (stat_ern)
terms.definition: A pentagon stat on a 1-10 integer scale measuring how well a specific school+major combination pays, blending program-level earnings with occupation-level wage position. Computed as ROUND(1.0 + 9.0 * (0.6 * scorecard_percentile + 0.4 * occupation_wage_percentile)), where scorecard_percentile is the CIP Family Earnings Rank (BT-022) and occupation_wage_percentile is the Wage Percentile Overall (BT-048). The 60/40 weighting prioritizes school-specific outcomes (a Stanford CS grad earns more than average) while grounding in occupation-level reality. Null if either input percentile is null.

terms.name: Return on Investment (stat_roi)
terms.definition: A pentagon stat on a 1-10 integer scale measuring how efficiently a program converts debt into earnings. Derived from the Debt-to-Earnings Ratio (BT-019) via piecewise linear interpolation: ratio <= 0.25 maps to 10 (excellent), 0.25-0.75 maps to 10-8, 0.75-1.5 maps to 8-5, 1.5-2.5 maps to 5-3, 2.5-4.0 maps to 3-1, and > 4.0 maps to 1. Thresholds are informed by Department of Education Gainful Employment guidance. Null if debt_to_earnings_annual is null.

terms.name: AI Resilience (stat_res)
terms.definition: A pentagon stat on a 1-10 integer scale measuring how resistant an occupation is to AI-driven automation and displacement. Derived from the Karpathy AI Exposure Score (BT-094) using the formula MIN(11 - exposure_score, 10). A high RES score means the occupation is resilient to AI disruption; a low score means it is highly susceptible. The inverse relationship to the source exposure score means stat_res + boss_ai_score = 11 for all occupations with exposure_score >= 1.

terms.name: Boss Fight Score
terms.definition: A game-mechanic concept in the FutureProof system where career challenges are personified as 'bosses' that a student must overcome. Each boss is scored 1-10 where higher means the boss is stronger (worse for the student). Five bosses exist: AI (placeholder, null), Student Loans (inverse of ROI), Market (from BLS employment health), Burnout (from O*NET work context), and Ceiling (from wage position within education tier). Boss scores are the adversarial counterpart to pentagon stats — stats measure strengths while bosses measure challenges.

terms.name: Program Career Path
terms.definition: The core Gold data product entity representing a single school+major+career combination in the consumable.program_career_paths table. Grain is unitid x cipcode x soc_code (one row per institution x program x occupation). Created by joining College Scorecard career outcomes to BLS and O*NET occupation data via the CIP-SOC crosswalk using 4-digit CIP prefix matching. Contains all five pentagon stats, all five boss fight scores, program-level earnings/debt context, occupation-level wage/growth context, and data quality indicators (match_quality, overall_confidence). Estimated 150,000-500,000 rows due to CIP prefix join fan-out.

terms.name: Boss AI Score
terms.definition: A boss fight score on a 1-10 integer scale representing the strength of the AI automation threat to an occupation. Derived from the Karpathy AI Exposure Score (BT-094) using the formula MAX(exposure_score, 1). Higher score means greater AI threat (worse for the student). Every boss has at least strength 1. A Karpathy exposure_score of 10 maps directly to boss_ai_score 10.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/raw-ingest-karpathy-ai-exposure.md (Zone 3: Gold Transformations, Boss AI Score Derivation)

terms.name: Boss Loans Score
terms.definition: A boss fight score on a 1-10 integer scale representing the severity of the student debt burden for a given program. Computed as 11 minus stat_roi (BT-079), making it the exact inverse of the ROI pentagon stat. A program with excellent ROI (stat_roi=10) has an easy Loans boss (score=1), while a program with terrible ROI (stat_roi=1) faces a devastating Loans boss (score=10). Null when stat_roi is null.

terms.name: Boss Ceiling Score
terms.definition: A boss fight score on a 1-10 integer scale measuring how constrained an occupation's earnings are within its education tier. Computed as ROUND(10.0 - 9.0 * wage_percentile_education_tier) where wage_percentile_education_tier (BT-049) is the occupation's relative wage position among careers requiring the same education level. Low earners within their tier face a strong Ceiling boss (high score, stuck below the ceiling). High earners face a weak boss (low score, already pushed past it). Null when wage_percentile_education_tier is null.

terms.name: CIP Prefix Match
terms.definition: The join technique used in the Gold FutureProof Engine to bridge College Scorecard program data (which uses 4-digit CIP codes in XX.XX format) to the CIP-SOC crosswalk (which uses 6-digit CIP codes in XX.XXXX format). Implemented as LEFT(crosswalk.cipcode, 5) to truncate 6-digit CIP to its 4-digit prefix, enabling joins where strict matching would produce 0% match rate. Achieves 91.0% CIP code coverage and 97.1% row coverage. Introduces fan-out: a single Scorecard CIP may match multiple 6-digit crosswalk CIPs, each mapping to different SOC codes, requiring dedup on the final grain (unitid, cipcode, soc_code).
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-futureproof-engine.md (CRITICAL: CIP Granularity Mismatch Resolution section)

terms.name: Stats Available Count
terms.definition: An integer data quality indicator (0-5) on each row of the consumable.program_career_paths table counting how many of the five pentagon stats (stat_ern, stat_roi, stat_res, stat_grw, stat_hmn) are non-null. For the hackathon MVP, the maximum is 4 since stat_res is always null. Feeds into the overall_confidence derivation. A count of 0 means no stats could be computed (scorecard-only rows with no occupation data and missing earnings).

terms.name: Bosses Available Count
terms.definition: An integer data quality indicator (0-5) on each row of the consumable.program_career_paths table counting how many of the five boss fight scores (boss_ai_score, boss_loans_score, boss_market_score, boss_burnout_score, boss_ceiling_score) are non-null. For the hackathon MVP, the maximum is 4 since boss_ai_score is always null. Feeds into the overall_confidence derivation alongside stats_available_count.

terms.name: Overall Confidence (FutureProof Engine)
terms.definition: A three-level data quality classification assigned to every row in the consumable.program_career_paths table, derived from the combination of stats_available_count and match_quality. 'high': stats_available_count >= 4 AND match_quality = 'full' (the row has most stats computed and all data sources joined successfully). 'medium': stats_available_count >= 2 AND match_quality contains 'partial' (some stats available but not all data sources joined). 'low': stats_available_count < 2 OR match_quality = 'scorecard_only' (minimal data — only Scorecard earnings without occupation-level enrichment). Distinct from BT-024 (career outcomes confidence based on cohort size), BT-052 (occupation confidence based on wage/broad/catchall), and BT-071 (O*NET work profile confidence based on survey coverage).

terms.name: Career Branch
terms.definition: A Gold data product entity in the consumable.career_branches table representing an enriched occupational similarity pair. Each row connects a source occupation to a related (branch target) occupation, carrying both occupations' full stat profiles (GRW, HMN, Burnout, wage) and pre-computed deltas between them. Grain is soc_code x related_soc_code. Enriches the Silver-zone career transitions (BT-060) with Gold-level scores so the frontend can render branch tree visualizations without additional queries. Estimated 15,944 rows (1:1 with Silver career_transitions).

terms.name: Stat Delta
terms.definition: A derived numeric difference between a branch target occupation's score and the source occupation's score for the same metric, computed in the consumable.career_branches table. Four deltas are produced: grw_delta (related_grw - source_grw), hmn_delta (related_hmn - source_hmn), burnout_delta (related_burnout - source_burnout), and wage_delta (related_wage - source_wage). Positive values mean the branch target scores higher on that metric. For stat scores, range is -9 to +9 (since stats are 1-10). For wage_delta, range is unconstrained. Null if either source or target value is null. Enables quick comparison of career branch trade-offs without recomputation.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/gold-futureproof-engine.md (Table 2 schema: grw_delta, hmn_delta, burnout_delta, wage_delta fields)

terms.name: Branch Has Full Data
terms.definition: A derived boolean flag on each row of the consumable.career_branches table indicating whether the branch target (related) occupation has both BLS occupation profile data and O*NET work profile data available. True means the related occupation's GRW, HMN, Burnout, and wage scores are all potentially computable; False means one or both data sources are missing for the related occupation. Enables the frontend to visually distinguish branches with complete comparison data from those with partial profiles.

terms.name: Match Quality (Gold Engine)
terms.definition: A derived categorical classification assigned to every row in the consumable.program_career_paths table, computed at Gold time from actual join results rather than from the Silver crosswalk's pre-computed flags. Four possible values: 'full' (both BLS and O*NET joins succeeded — all occupation-level data available), 'partial_no_onet' (BLS join succeeded but O*NET did not — missing HMN and Burnout), 'partial_no_bls' (O*NET join succeeded but BLS did not — missing GRW and Market), 'scorecard_only' (neither BLS nor O*NET join succeeded — only program-level earnings data available). CRITICAL: this must NOT use the Silver crosswalk's has_scorecard_match flag, which is FALSE for all rows due to the CIP granularity mismatch. The Gold engine derives match_quality from whether occupation_profiles and onet_work_profiles rows actually exist for the joined soc_code.

terms.name: AI Exposure Score (Karpathy)
terms.definition: An LLM-generated estimate (0-10) of how much current AI technology will reshape a given occupation, from Andrej Karpathy's US Job Market Visualizer project. The score considers both direct automation (AI replacing human tasks) and indirect productivity effects (AI making workers dramatically more efficient). A score of 0 means minimal AI impact; 10 means the occupation's core tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven change. Important: a high score does NOT predict job disappearance -- it predicts that the nature of the work will change significantly. Scores were produced by Gemini Flash (an LLM) evaluating full BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook descriptions against a structured rubric. They are estimates, not empirical measurements. Known limitation: the LLM scoring its own replaceability of jobs introduces self-referential bias.

terms.name: AI Exposure Rationale
terms.definition: A 2-3 sentence LLM-generated explanation of the key factors driving an occupation's AI exposure score. Each rationale is unique and describes why the occupation scored as it did -- for example, whether the job involves heavy computer-based work (higher exposure) or hands-on physical tasks (lower exposure). This is a display field served to end users in the FutureProof frontend as part of the Fight AI boss narrative, providing interpretable context beyond the numeric score. Sourced from Karpathy's scoring pipeline (Gemini Flash output).

terms.name: SOC Resolution Method
terms.definition: A classification indicating how a Karpathy AI exposure row's SOC code was determined during Silver zone transformation. Four possible values: 'direct' (SOC was present in source data), 'title_match' (SOC was null in source but resolved by matching occupation title against base.bls_ooh), 'broad_expansion' (row was generated by expanding a broad SOC code XX-XXX0 to its constituent detailed codes), 'unresolved' (no SOC could be determined -- row preserved for completeness but cannot join downstream).

terms.name: BLS Match Flag
terms.definition: A boolean flag on each row in base.karpathy_ai_exposure indicating whether the row's SOC code was found in base.bls_ooh. True means the occupation exists in the FutureProof BLS ingest and can be joined downstream. False means the SOC code is valid but absent from our BLS data (possible SOC vintage mismatch or different BLS snapshot). Used as a filter criterion at Gold -- only bls_match=true rows are promoted to consumable.ai_exposure.

terms.name: Regional Price Parity (RPP)
terms.definition: A BEA index measuring the price level of goods and services in a state relative to the national average (set to 100.0). An RPP of 110 means prices are 10% higher than the national average; an RPP of 88 means prices are 12% lower. Used to adjust national salary figures to local purchasing power. Published annually by the Bureau of Economic Analysis in the Regional Economic Accounts (table SARPP, LineCode=1 = All Items). The index covers all 50 states plus the District of Columbia. A single RPP is a state-level aggregate, not a per-person or per-household measurement.

terms.name: Purchasing Power Multiplier
terms.definition: The factor by which a national salary is multiplied to reflect local purchasing power in a given state. Derived as 100 / RPP (Regional Price Parity). A multiplier of 0.90 means $50K nationally buys only $45K worth of goods locally (high-cost state). A multiplier of 1.14 means $50K nationally buys $57K worth of goods locally (low-cost state). The multiplier is a pure mathematical inverse of BT-098 and carries the same vintage and state grain. FutureProof computes this at the Silver zone and carries it through Gold and MCP so the frontend and Gemma agent can apply salary adjustments without client-side math.

terms.name: State FIPS Code
terms.definition: Two-digit Federal Information Processing Standards (FIPS) numeric code identifying a U.S. state or DC, maintained by NIST/ANSI. Canonical state identifier used across all government statistical publications. Always stored as a zero-padded 2-character string (e.g., '06' for California, '19' for Iowa, '11' for District of Columbia). Intentional gaps exist at codes 03, 07, 14, 43, and 52 because those FIPS codes were never assigned. State FIPS is the sole stable join key across BEA, BLS, Census, and other federal statistical products and is the grain key for the BEA RPP table.

terms.name: State Name
terms.definition: Full English name of a U.S. state or the District of Columbia, matching USPS canonical form. Used as the display label in user-facing components (the MCP tool response, the frontend salary-adjustment UI, and Gemma's narrative output). 'District of Columbia' is spelled out in full rather than abbreviated as 'DC'. The 51 values are distinct and 1:1 with BT-100 State FIPS Code; state name is display-only and never used as a join key.
terms.source_reference: USPS Publication 28 / U.S. Census Bureau state name list

terms.name: RPP Data Year
terms.definition: Calendar year for which a Regional Price Parity index applies. BEA publishes annually and values are static within a year; year is provenance, not a temporal dimension. A row labeled with the wrong data_year silently misrepresents the vintage of every downstream purchasing-power figure, so data_year is the P0 provenance guardrail that governs annual-refresh replacement semantics. The current FutureProof load pins data_year = 2024, matching the BEA 2024 RPP release published in February 2026.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/raw-ingest-bea-rpp.md (Zone 1: Raw Ingest, annual refresh semantics)

terms.name: USPS State Abbreviation
terms.definition: Two-letter uppercase postal abbreviation assigned by the United States Postal Service for each of the 50 U.S. states and the District of Columbia (e.g., 'CA' for California, 'IA' for Iowa, 'DC' for the District of Columbia). The codes are a closed set of 51 values defined in USPS Publication 28 (Postal Addressing Standards, Appendix B), used on mailing addresses and adopted as a compact display identifier across federal statistical products. In FutureProof, the USPS abbreviation is the frontend display identifier (e.g., state selector dropdown values) and the state-selection key used in the MCP tool signature for the purchasing-power-adjustment tool. It is 1:1 with BT-100 State FIPS Code and BT-101 State Name; FIPS remains the internal join key while USPS is surfaced at the edge for human readability and URL/API compactness.

terms.name: Census Region
terms.definition: One of four groupings of U.S. states defined by the U.S. Census Bureau — 'Northeast', 'Midwest', 'South', and 'West' — used to aggregate states for regional comparisons and statistical publications. The regions partition all 50 states plus the District of Columbia into mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive sets. Notable quirk: the District of Columbia is placed in the 'South' region by Census convention despite its Northeast-adjacent geography and despite exhibiting a Regional Price Parity profile closer to Northeast metros than to Southern states — consumers of this field should not assume Census Region correlates cleanly with cost-of-living tier. The four-region scheme is distinct from the finer-grained nine Census Divisions; FutureProof uses regions only (not divisions) because the frontend regional comparison view groups states at the four-region level.
terms.source_reference: U.S. Census Bureau, Statistical Abstract / Geographic Terms and Concepts — https://www.census.gov/programs-surveys/economic-census/guidance-geographies/levels.html#par_textimage_34

terms.name: Data Verification Status
terms.definition: A per-row provenance label on the silver_base_bea_rpp table indicating whether a given state's Regional Price Parity value originated from an authoritative BEA publication or from a plausible primary-agent estimate pending a live BEA API refresh. The field is a closed enumeration with exactly two values: (1) 'bea_official' — the RPP value was sourced from the BEA Regional Economic Accounts SARPP table, LineCode=1 (All Items), 2024 vintage. At the current hackathon snapshot this value applies to exactly 8 states and jurisdictions: California, Hawaii, District of Columbia, New Jersey, Arkansas, Mississippi, Iowa, and Oklahoma. (2) 'estimate' — the RPP value is a primary-agent-supplied plausible value pending refresh; at the current hackathon snapshot this value applies to the other 43 states. Total rows = 51 (50 states + DC). When the post-hackathon BEA API refresh completes, the Silver transformer will replace all estimated values with official BEA values and every row will become 'bea_official'. The allow-list of 8 verified states is hard-coded in the Silver transformer and cross-referenced against the EDA notebook at governance/eda/raw-bea-rpp-eda.md. Downstream consumers (Gold, MCP, frontend) must surface this label so users can distinguish provisional from authoritative purchasing-power adjustments; the Gemma narrative agent is required to disclose estimate provenance in its generated copy.
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/silver-base-bea-rpp.md (Zone 2: Silver Transformations, verification_status derivation) — allow-list cross-referenced to governance/eda/raw-bea-rpp-eda.md

terms.name: Cost Tier
terms.definition: A five-bucket classification of state-level cost of living derived from the Regional Price Parity (BT-098) rpp_all_items value. The five enumerated values are: (1) 'very_high' — RPP >= 108 (includes California 110.7, Hawaii 110.0, District of Columbia 109.9, New Jersey 108.8); (2) 'high' — 103 <= RPP < 108; (3) 'average' — 97 <= RPP < 103; (4) 'low' — 91 <= RPP < 97; (5) 'very_low' — RPP < 91 (includes Arkansas 86.9, Mississippi 87.0, Iowa 87.8, Oklahoma 87.8). Breakpoints are left-closed (inclusive on the lower bound, exclusive on the upper bound). Used downstream for frontend color coding of the state map and salary-adjustment UI, for Fight Location Lock boss-difficulty selection in the gameplay layer, and for broad regional narrative prompts issued to the Gemma agent. Important: cost_tier is a FutureProof editorial classification of the BEA rpp_all_items value — it is not a direct BEA publication. The breakpoints are a FutureProof editorial choice calibrated to produce approximately five buckets with reasonable density across the 51-row state+DC universe, not a BEA-defined cost-of-living category system. Any change to the breakpoints must be treated as a breaking change to downstream consumers.
used_in_models: mcp-regional-price-parities (forward-looking — any MCP spec consuming cost_tier)

terms.name: Adjusted Salary
terms.definition: A national salary figure converted to its equivalent purchasing-power value in a given U.S. state by multiplying the national salary by that state's Purchasing Power Multiplier (BT-099). Formula: adjusted_salary = national_salary * (100 / state_RPP) = national_salary * purchasing_power_multiplier. For example, a $50,000 national salary in California (RPP 110.7) yields an adjusted salary of approximately $45,167, while the same $50,000 in Arkansas (RPP 86.9) yields approximately $57,537. The Gold table consumable.regional_price_parities pre-computes four specific salary benchmarks — adjusted_30k, adjusted_50k, adjusted_75k, and adjusted_100k — so the frontend and Gemma narrative agent can display purchasing-power-adjusted values without performing client-side math. Values are stored as doubles rounded to two decimal places (cents precision); sub-cent precision is considered false precision for a state-level aggregate index and is explicitly disallowed. Adjusted salary is a pure derivation from BT-098 and BT-099 and inherits their vintage (data_year) and state grain.
used_in_models: mcp-regional-price-parities (forward-looking — any MCP spec consuming adjusted salary)

terms.name: Cost of Attendance (COA)
terms.definition: The total annual cost of attending an institution, including tuition, fees, books, supplies, room & board, and living expenses. This is the 'sticker price' before any financial aid, grants, or scholarships are applied. College Scorecard publishes two variants — COSTT4_A for academic-year institutions and COSTT4_P for program-year institutions — which are mutually exclusive at the institution level (no institution reports both). Silver produces a unified cost_of_attendance_annual as COALESCE(costt4_a, costt4_p). Derived upstream from IPEDS institutional characteristics and student financial aid components. Observed range in the 2023 vintage is approximately $6,362–$87,804 (median $30,288).
terms.source_reference: https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/data/documentation/ (COSTT4_A, COSTT4_P fields); IPEDS Institutional Characteristics (IC) and Student Financial Aid (SFA) components

terms.name: Net Price
terms.definition: The average annual cost students actually pay after subtracting grants and scholarships from the cost of attendance. This is the amount students must cover through savings, earnings, and/or loans, and serves as the ROI denominator input in the FutureProof formula earnings / (net_price * 4 * loan_pct). College Scorecard publishes two variants based on institutional control: NPT4_PUB for public institutions (control=1) and NPT4_PRIV for private institutions (control IN (2,3), both nonprofit and for-profit). Silver unifies these into a single net_price_annual column keyed on institution_control. Net price can legitimately be negative when average grant aid exceeds total cost of attendance (e.g., Skyline College at -$1,180). Observed 2023 ranges: NPT4_PUB -$1,180 to $32,598; NPT4_PRIV $1,525 to $77,180.
terms.source_reference: https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/data/documentation/ (NPT4_PUB, NPT4_PRIV fields); IPEDS Student Financial Aid (SFA) component

terms.name: Net Price by Income Quintile
terms.definition: Net price broken down by family income bracket. College Scorecard reports five brackets: Q1 = $0–$30K (NPT41), Q2 = $30K–$48K (NPT42), Q3 = $48K–$75K (NPT43), Q4 = $75K–$110K (NPT44), Q5 = $110K+ (NPT45). Each bracket is published separately for public (NPT41_PUB..NPT45_PUB) and private (NPT41_PRIV..NPT45_PRIV) institutions, yielding ten source fields total. Lower-income families typically receive more need-based aid, resulting in lower net prices; by the Q5 bracket, net price is generally closest to the full sticker price at a given school. The monotonicity invariant net_price_q1 ≤ net_price_q5 is enforced as a P1 data-quality rule (where both values are non-null). Quintile-level net price can be negative at high-endowment institutions for the Q1 bracket (e.g., MIT Q1 at -$4,129).
terms.source_reference: https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/data/documentation/ (NPT41_PUB through NPT45_PUB and NPT41_PRIV through NPT45_PRIV fields); IPEDS Student Financial Aid (SFA) component

terms.name: Net Price (4-Year Total)
terms.definition: The estimated total net cost of completing a four-year bachelor's degree at an institution, derived as net_price_annual (BT-111) multiplied by 4. This is a FutureProof-specific derivation intended for at-a-glance degree-cost comparison and for the follow-up ROI formula earnings / (net_price * 4 * loan_pct) landing in spec roi-formula-cost-of-attendance.md. The derivation assumes a stable annual net price across four enrollment years and does not account for tuition inflation, aid-eligibility changes, or time-to-degree variance beyond four years. Because it is a pure ×4 transform of BT-111, it inherits all nullness, range, and negativity properties: observed 2023 Silver range is -$4,720 (Skyline College) to $308,720 (Galen Health Institutes-Asheville), with median $75,960 and mean $78,683 across 2,233 non-null institutions. The Silver invariant |net_price_4yr - (net_price_annual * 4)| ≤ $1 is enforced at P0 and observed violation-free (IEEE-754 exact).

terms.name: Institution Control
terms.definition: The governance and funding classification of a postsecondary institution, indicating whether it is publicly funded, privately funded on a nonprofit basis, or privately funded on a for-profit basis. IPEDS and College Scorecard publish this as a numeric code in the raw CONTROL field: 1 = Public, 2 = Private nonprofit, 3 = Private for-profit. Silver maps the code to its string label (institution_control: 'Public' | 'Private nonprofit' | 'Private for-profit') and uses the value to route the correct net-price field (NPT4_PUB for control=1, NPT4_PRIV for control IN (2,3)) when constructing the unified net_price_annual (BT-111) and net_price_q1..q5 (BT-112). Observed 2023 Silver distribution across 3,039 bachelor's-granting institutions: 867 Public (28.53%), 1,754 Private nonprofit (57.72%), 418 Private for-profit (13.75%); zero nulls, zero out-of-domain values. Closes the 2026-04-06 insight-report recommendation to surface institution type on consumable.career_outcomes (previously 100% null).

terms.name: Tuition and Fees
terms.definition: The published annual tuition charge plus required institutional fees for a full-time undergraduate student at a postsecondary institution, excluding room, board, books, and other living expenses. This term covers both residency variants published by College Scorecard: in-state tuition and fees (TUITIONFEE_IN, stored as tuition_in_state) — the rate charged to students who are residents of the state where a public institution is located, and out-of-state tuition and fees (TUITIONFEE_OUT, stored as tuition_out_of_state) — the rate charged to non-resident students at public institutions, or the single published rate at most private institutions (where in-state and out-of-state are typically equal). Tuition and fees is a component of cost of attendance (BT-110) but not the same as net price (BT-111), which nets out grant aid. Used in FutureProof primarily for display and receipt purposes, not ROI computation. Observed 2023 Bronze range across both variants: approximately $600 to $69,330.
terms.source_reference: https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/data/documentation/ (TUITIONFEE_IN, TUITIONFEE_OUT fields); IPEDS Institutional Characteristics (IC) component

terms.name: Room and Board
terms.definition: The combined annual charge for student housing and meal-plan expenses at a postsecondary institution. College Scorecard publishes two variants: on-campus room and board (ROOMBOARD_ON, stored as room_board_on_campus) — the institutionally-provided housing + meal-plan cost for students living in dorms or similar on-campus housing, and off-campus room and board (ROOMBOARD_OFF, stored as room_board_off_campus) — an estimated equivalent cost for students living off-campus but not with family. Room and board is a component of cost of attendance (BT-110) and is exclusive of tuition and fees (BT-115), books and supplies, and other personal expenses. FutureProof lands only the on-campus variant on consumable.career_outcomes (for display/receipts); the off-campus variant remains in Silver for future use. Observed 2023 Bronze ranges: on-campus approximately $1,000–$29,874; off-campus approximately $2,001–$39,100.
terms.source_reference: https://collegescorecard.ed.gov/data/documentation/ (ROOMBOARD_ON, ROOMBOARD_OFF fields); IPEDS Institutional Characteristics (IC) component

terms.name: Related Work Experience
terms.definition: O*NET measure of the typical prior work experience required to enter an occupation, expressed as a percent frequency distribution across 11 ordered duration categories ranging from 'None' to 'Over 10 years' (category midpoints: 0, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 9, 10, 12 years). Sourced from the O*NET Data Collection Program incumbent survey (element 3.A.1, scale 'RW' — Related Work Experience). Each occupation's distribution sums to 100%. FutureProof collapses this distribution into a single scalar experience_years_typical per occupation using a weighted-median aggregation over the category midpoints, then bins that scalar into an Experience Tier (BT-118). This is the authoritative source for how many years of prior work experience a career typically requires, distinct from education (which O*NET measures on a separate scale) and on-the-job training.
terms.source_reference: O*NET 30.2 Database, 'Education, Training, and Experience.txt', element 3.A.1 / scale RW (Related Work Experience); https://www.onetcenter.org/dictionary/30.2/text/education_training_experience.html; see also governance/approvals/onet-experience-requirements-open-decisions.md (2026-04-16 human approval of category midpoints and weighted-median aggregation).

terms.name: Experience Tier
terms.definition: FutureProof-derived classification of occupations by typical experience requirement, based on the experience_years_typical scalar derived from O*NET Related Work Experience (BT-117). Four-valued enumeration: 'entry' (0 ≤ years ≤ 1), 'early' (1 < years ≤ 4), 'mid' (4 < years ≤ 8), 'senior' (years > 8). Drives default filtering and 'decade bucketing' in the career evolution tree (Stage 3 branching graph), allowing users to see which downstream careers are reachable now vs. after several years of experience. Thresholds were human-approved 2026-04-16; see governance/approvals/onet-experience-requirements-open-decisions.md.

terms.name: Athletic Subsidy Ratio
terms.definition: The fraction of an institution's intercollegiate athletics expenses that is not covered by athletic revenue, expressed as `(total_athletic_expenses - total_athletic_revenue) / NULLIF(total_athletic_expenses, 0)`. Positive values mean the rest of the institution's budget subsidizes athletics; near-zero values mean the program is approximately self-sustaining; negative values mean the program is profitable. Computed at Silver (`base.eada`) from the EADA institution-grand-total fields. **Note on EADA convention:** at the grand-total grain, EADA requires reported revenues to be at least equal to expenses (any deficit is conventionally booked as institutional support, a separate column not currently ingested). The grand-total ratio is therefore near zero everywhere; the canonical 'athletics loses money' insight requires the unbundled `direct_institutional_support` field which is out of scope for this Bronze ingest. Carried through to `consumable.institution_aura` as a context column only — explicitly **excluded** from the `aura_score` composite per spec §2 Decision 11 (it encodes a normative judgment that overlaps with ROI / ERN and would remove the analytical tension between aura and the pentagon).
terms.source_reference: docs/specs/full-pipeline-eada.md §5 Base Transformations + §6 Consumable Schema; governance/eda/full-pipeline-eada-raw-eda.md (revenue-equals-expense observation at grand-total grain).
terms.steward_notes: Final term_id is @bs:data-steward's call. The spec proposes the alphabetic form `BT-EAD-ATHLETIC-SUBSIDY-RATIO`; the existing glossary uses numeric IDs (BT-001..BT-118), so the steward may prefer to re-key as BT-119. Companion BT-AUR-AURA-SCORE and BT-AUR-COVERAGE-TIER are deferred to gold-zone work and are NOT added in this update.

terms.name: Instruction Expenses
terms.definition: An institution's total annual expenses for the colleges, schools, departments, and other instructional divisions of the institution — including faculty salaries, instructional materials, departmental research, and public service that is not separately budgeted. The IPEDS dictionary definition (FARM ¶703.1) is byte-equivalent across all three forms: F1A `F1C011` (public, GASB — Part C functional expenses), F2 `F2E011` (private nonprofit, FASB — Section E functional expenses), F3 `F3E011` (private for-profit; the `1` suffix denotes 'Total amount'). Drives `base.ipeds_finance.instruction_per_fte` and is the denominator of `marketing_ratio`. **EDA-observed (FY23):** range $0–$3.5B (Stanford); p50 $15.2M; 365 institutions exceed $100M (R1 anchors). 5 rows at $0 are real reported zeros (small F2/F3 institutions booking teaching costs under academic support — allowed by RAW-IPF-005).
terms.source_reference: https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/use-the-data (IPEDS Finance Survey dictionary, FARM ¶703.1); docs/specs/full-pipeline-ipeds-finance.md §3 column table (column codes locked v1.3); governance/eda/full-pipeline-ipeds-finance-raw-eda.md §2 (column codes verified at byte level against FY23 dictionary varlists)
terms.steward_notes: Final term_id is @bs:data-steward's call. The spec proposes the alphabetic form `BT-IPF-INSTRUCTION-EXPENSES`; the existing glossary uses numeric IDs (BT-001..BT-118), so the steward may prefer to re-key as BT-119+ alongside the BT-EAD-ATHLETIC-SUBSIDY-RATIO and other BT-IPF-* proposals.

terms.name: Institutional Support Expenses
terms.definition: An institution's total annual expenses for executive management, fiscal operations, public relations, fundraising, legal services, administrative computing, and similar administrative functions. The IPEDS dictionary definition (FARM ¶703.9) is byte-equivalent across all three forms: F1A `F1C071` (public, GASB), F2 `F2E061` (private nonprofit, FASB), F3 `F3E03C1` (private for-profit; post-2014-15 schedule). Often a proxy for 'marketing and administration overhead' in budget-transparency analyses — at for-profits (F3) it routinely exceeds instruction spending (median `marketing_ratio` ~1.06), the marketing-heavy signal `marketing_ratio` is designed to surface. **F3 reporting note:** EDA refuted the pre-v1.3 hypothesis that F3 omits this field — `F3E03C1` is populated for 100% of FY23 F3 institutions. **EDA-observed (FY23):** range $0–$1.23B; p50 $9.4M; 100% non-null. Drives `base.ipeds_finance.institutional_support_per_fte` and is the numerator of `marketing_ratio`.
terms.source_reference: https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/use-the-data (IPEDS Finance Survey dictionary, FARM ¶703.9); docs/specs/full-pipeline-ipeds-finance.md §3 column table (F3 column locked v1.3 to `F3E03C1`); governance/eda/full-pipeline-ipeds-finance-raw-eda.md §3, §7 (form-coalescing semantic equivalence; F3 NULL refutation)
terms.steward_notes: Final term_id is @bs:data-steward's call. The spec proposes the alphabetic form `BT-IPF-INSTITUTIONAL-SUPPORT-EXPENSES`; the existing glossary uses numeric IDs (BT-001..BT-118), so the steward may prefer to re-key as BT-119+ alongside the BT-EAD-ATHLETIC-SUBSIDY-RATIO and other BT-IPF-* proposals.

terms.name: Endowment Value
terms.definition: The end-of-year market value of an institution's endowment funds, reported on the IPEDS Finance Survey. Sourced from F1A `F1H02` (public, GASB) and F2 `F2H02` (private nonprofit, FASB). **Not applicable to for-profit institutions (F3)** — F3 has no `F3H` family on its finance schedule (for-profits do not maintain endowments by design); `endowment_value` is structurally NULL for 100% of F3 rows (277/277 in FY23), not missing data. Per IPEDS reporting, underwater funds report `0`, never negative — `endowment_value ≥ 0` where non-null is enforced by RAW-IPF-007. The 128 rows with `endowment_value = $0` (where non-null) are real reported values (small institutions and underwater funds), not suppressions. Drives `base.ipeds_finance.endowment_per_fte`. **EDA-observed (FY23):** range $0–$50.7B (Stanford); p50 $46.0M; 76.0% non-null overall (24% NULL is mostly structural F3 + ~16% small F2 institutions that don't maintain endowments).
terms.source_reference: https://nces.ed.gov/ipeds/use-the-data (IPEDS Finance Survey dictionary, F1A Part H / F2 Section H); docs/specs/full-pipeline-ipeds-finance.md §3 column table (F3 N/A locked v1.3); governance/eda/full-pipeline-ipeds-finance-raw-eda.md §7 (F3 100% structural NULL on endowment, refutes nothing — confirms expected behavior)
